text: Providing well-calibrated probabilistic predictions for intelligent decision making is a fundamental problem in AI, and almost all the popular supervised learning models fail to produce well-calibrated predictions. We present a novel non-parametric calibration method for deep networks — Bayesian Binning into Quantiles (BBQ) — that calibrated the predictions of deep network binary predictors (i.e. classifiers with two output classes) and improved the accuracy of the network itself. BBQed predictors were better calibrated than state of the art calibration methods like Platt scaling and isotonic regression when measuring calibration via ECE and MCE, while still maintaining, if not improving upon the network's classification performance. The BBQ calibration method transforms the calibration problem into an optimal binning one; but does away with several well known drawbacks of other histogram binning calibration techniques such as: - Selection of fixed bin counts. - Overfitting small data sets and being particularly sensitive to outliers of diverse nature that populate these sets. BBQ achieves this remarkable advancement by drawing samples uniformly at random from a set of several identical frequency bin calibrated models and then via Bayesian model averaging. Experiments conducted on different data sets on the real and simulated data sets showed their merits and strong calibration performances.
